title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about the psychology of decision-making under pressure. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Thinking Trap
content.subtitle: A magazine article exploring how our minds can mislead us when we need them most.
content.text: Every day, without realising it, we make thousands of decisions. Most of them are trivial — what to eat for breakfast, which route to take to work. But when the stakes rise and the pressure mounts, something curious happens to the human mind. [31] Rather than becoming sharper, as we might expect, our thinking frequently becomes more rigid, more prone to shortcuts, and less able to consider alternative viewpoints. [/31] Psychologists have a name for this: cognitive narrowing. || The phenomenon was first documented in clinical settings, where researchers noticed that patients facing stressful medical decisions consistently chose the first acceptable option presented to them, regardless of whether it was the best one. This tendency — known as 'satisficing' — is not a sign of laziness or low intelligence. [32] It is, in fact, an ancient and once-useful survival mechanism, a way of avoiding paralysis when a predator was near and any action was better than none. [/32] In modern life, however, this shortcut can lead us to make choices we later regret. || Dr Priya Menon, a cognitive psychologist at University College London, has spent fifteen years studying how people reason under pressure. Her research suggests that the quality of a decision is less determined by intelligence than by the conditions in which the decision is made. 'Give someone a complex problem in a calm, well-lit room with no time pressure,' she told me, 'and they'll usually find a reasonable solution. Put them under a deadline, add an audience, and let them feel that something important is at stake — the decision quality drops dramatically.' || [33] One of Dr Menon's most striking findings is that people who believe themselves to be good decision-makers are often among the worst performers in high-pressure scenarios. [/33] Overconfidence in one's own reasoning, she argues, prevents people from seeking outside perspectives or questioning their first instincts — the very habits that protect against cognitive narrowing. || So what can be done? Dr Menon advocates for what she calls 'structured reflection', a deliberate pause built into any significant decision-making process. This involves identifying your initial instinct, then actively searching for at least two pieces of evidence that contradict it before committing to any course of action. [34] It sounds deceptively simple, but in practice, most people find the process deeply uncomfortable. [/34] Admitting that your first thought might be wrong runs counter to the confidence we rely on to act decisively. || There is also the question of emotion. A popular misconception holds that good decisions are purely rational ones — that the ideal mind is cold and analytical. But neuroscientist Antonio Damasio famously demonstrated that patients with damage to the emotional centres of the brain, while remaining intellectually intact, became almost entirely unable to make practical decisions. [35] Emotion, it turns out, is not the enemy of good judgement — it is a necessary component of it. [/35] || The challenge, then, is not to eliminate emotional responses but to become aware of them. [36] Dr Menon describes this awareness as 'emotional literacy' — the ability to recognise when a feeling is informing your judgement in a helpful way, and when it is simply distorting it. [/36]

questions.question: The writer opens the article by pointing out that
options: A) people are naturally skilled at making decisions under pressure.
B) high-pressure situations tend to improve our ability to focus.
C) human thinking often becomes less flexible when pressure increases.
D) most of the decisions we make daily are too unimportant to study.
questions.explanation: The text states that under pressure, thinking 'becomes more rigid, more prone to shortcuts, and less able to consider alternative viewpoints' — the opposite of what we might expect.

questions.question: The writer mentions 'satisficing' in order to
options: A) criticise people who fail to consider all their options carefully.
B) explain a decision-making shortcut as a product of human evolution.
C) argue that modern life is more demanding than life in the past.
D) show how stress can sometimes lead to better decisions.
questions.explanation: The writer frames satisficing as 'an ancient and once-useful survival mechanism', placing it in an evolutionary context rather than presenting it as a modern flaw.

questions.question: What surprising result did Dr Menon's research reveal about confident decision-makers?
options: A) They tend to perform better than average under time pressure.
B) They are less likely to be affected by cognitive narrowing.
C) Their self-assurance makes them perform worse in stressful situations.
D) They make fewer decisions than less confident individuals.
questions.explanation: Dr Menon found that people who consider themselves good decision-makers are 'often among the worst performers in high-pressure scenarios', as their overconfidence prevents helpful habits.

questions.question: The phrase 'deceptively simple' is used to suggest that structured reflection
options: A) is easier to understand than it is to actually carry out.
B) sounds complicated but is in fact straightforward in practice.
C) is only effective when combined with other decision-making tools.
D) works better for simple decisions than for complex ones.
questions.explanation: The writer says it 'sounds deceptively simple, but in practice, most people find the process deeply uncomfortable', meaning the simplicity of the description hides the real difficulty of doing it.

questions.question: The example of Damasio's patients is used to demonstrate that
options: A) brain injuries always lead to poor decision-making.
B) intellectual ability alone is not sufficient for making sound decisions.
C) emotional people tend to make better decisions than rational ones.
D) the brain processes emotion and reason in completely separate ways.
questions.explanation: Patients who were 'intellectually intact' but lacked emotional processing became unable to make practical decisions, showing intellect alone is not enough.

questions.question: Dr Menon's concept of 'emotional literacy' refers to the ability to
options: A) express feelings clearly when explaining a decision to others.
B) distinguish between emotions that help and those that hinder judgement.
C) remove emotional responses entirely from the decision-making process.
D) identify which emotions are more rational than others.
questions.explanation: Dr Menon defines emotional literacy as recognising when a feeling 'is informing your judgement in a helpful way' versus 'simply distorting it' — a distinction, not a removal.
